coredata.dc:title: Titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) MXene: A novel precursor to amphiphilic carbide-derived graphene quantum dots for fluorescent ink, light-emitting composite and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Li
Wu, Fangming
Yu, Jinhong
Deng, Qihuang
Zhang, Faai
Wang, Guan
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of fluorescent carbide-derived carbons (CDCs) remains a big challenge for material scientists. Herein, we report an unprecedented method for the synthesis of amphiphilic carbide-derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from layered Ti3C2Tx MXene using solvothermal treatment of Ti3C2Tx MXene in dimethylformamide (DMF). Our findings indicate that DMF can simultaneously act as reaction media and nitrogen-doping agent for the formation of highly fluorescent carbide-derived GQDs. The resulted GQDs with uniform size distribution exhibit excellent dispersibility in both hydrophilic solvents (e.g., water, DMF, and ethanol) and in hydrophobic solvents (e.g., chloroform and toluene). With their superior properties of bright and tunable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, good photostability and chemical inertness, the carbide-derived GQDs are promising for applications in fluorescent ink, light-emitting composites and cellular imaging as demonstrated in this paper. This work opens a new route towards the large-scale synthesis of high-quality GQDs, paving the way for their versatile applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from Ti3C2Tx MXene

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21364003 and No. 51663007), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (Grant No. 2014GXNSFCA118004), and project of outstanding young teachers' training in higher education institutions of Guangxi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Procedure for the fabrication of fluorescent amphiphilic GQDs by solvothermal treatment of Ti3C2Tx MXene in DMF. Photograph of as-prepared GQDs in DMF under daylight (b) and 365 nm UV lamp (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of the multilayer Ti3C2Tx MXene. Representative TEM images of delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene (b) and GQDs (c), insets of (b) and (c) are the photograph of delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene in DMF and high-resolution TEM image of GQDs, respectively. (d) AFM image of the GQDs, inset of (d) is the corresponding height profile along the dashed line. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Raman (a), FT-IR (b), XPS survey (c) and XPS Ti 2p (d) spectra of delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene (1) and GQDs (2). The black squares in (a) denote Ti component peaks. XPS C1s (d) and N 1s (e) spectra of GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of GQDs in various solvents under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamp (bottom). (b) UV–vis absorption (ABS), photoluminescence excitation (PLE) (λem = 405 nm), and photoluminescence (PL) (λex = 322 nm) spectra of GQDs in water. (c) PL spectra of aqueous dispersion of GQDs upon excitation at diverse wavelengths. (d) A typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of GQDs (λex = 322 nm) measured at 405 nm pH (e) and NaCl concentration (f) dependent fluorescent intensity ratio (I/I0) of the GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of a glass sheet after coating by the mixture of aqueous GQDs, glycol and PVP under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (b) Photograph of fluorescent words writing by using the mixture of aqueous GQDs, glycol and PVP as ink. (c) Photograph of a fluorescent “Monkey” pattern under 365 nm UV lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoluminescence (λex = 322 nm) spectra of PMMA–GQDs (a) and PVA–GQDs (b) composite films. The insets are the photographs of corresponding composite films under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV lamp (bottom), respectively. Photographs of the PAAm–GQDs hydrogel under daylight (c) and UV light (365 nm) (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CLSM images of MCF-7 cells after incubation with GQDs for 2 h by excitation at 488 nm (a), 364 nm (b), and under bright field (c). The scale bar is 20 μm. (d) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of GQDs, Alexa fluo 488, and fluorescein. I0 and I are the emission intensities of GQDs, Alexa fluo 488, and fluorescein without and with laser illumination for diverse time, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Cell viability of 293T cells (a) and MCF-7 cancer cells (b) after incubation with GQDs at varying concentrations for 4 and 24 h, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) MXene: A novel precursor to amphiphilic carbide-derived graphene quantum dots for fluorescent ink, light-emitting composite and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Ministry-Province Jointly-Constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metal and Featured Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Ministry-Province Jointly-Constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metal and Featured Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, Singapore 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of fluorescent carbide-derived carbons (CDCs) remains a big challenge for material scientists. Herein, we report an unprecedented method for the synthesis of amphiphilic carbide-derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from layered Ti3C2Tx MXene using solvothermal treatment of Ti3C2Tx MXene in dimethylformamide (DMF). Our findings indicate that DMF can simultaneously act as reaction media and nitrogen-doping agent for the formation of highly fluorescent carbide-derived GQDs. The resulted GQDs with uniform size distribution exhibit excellent dispersibility in both hydrophilic solvents (e.g., water, DMF, and ethanol) and in hydrophobic solvents (e.g., chloroform and toluene). With their superior properties of bright and tunable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, good photostability and chemical inertness, the carbide-derived GQDs are promising for applications in fluorescent ink, light-emitting composites and cellular imaging as demonstrated in this paper. This work opens a new route towards the large-scale synthesis of high-quality GQDs, paving the way for their versatile applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbide-derived carbons (CDCs) represent a unique group of carbon materials with fine structural controllability, and have found a wide range of structures associated applications [1–5]. In general, the formation of CDCs undergoes the removal of the metal atoms from the metal carbide templates, leading to atomic-level control of carbon structures. To date, many kinds of CDCs, including amorphous carbon, porous carbon, carbon nanotube, nanodiamond and graphene, have been produced using techniques such as halogenation, electrochemical etching, and hydrothermal treatment [6]. For instance, porous carbon and carbon/sulfur nanolaminates can be readily achieved through electrochemical etching of MAX phases carbides (where M represents an early transition metal, A corresponds to IIIA or IVA group elements, and X is C or N) [7,8]. Nevertheless, the synthesis of fluorescent CDCs from metal carbide is rarely reported.

As another important member of carbon materials, fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have captured increasing attention because of their superior properties such as tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and low toxicity as compared to traditional fluorescent materials (e.g., organic dyes) [9–11]. Fluorescent GQDs are promising for many applications including bioimaging, biosensing and optical devices and various techniques have been reported for the synthesis of GQDs [12–18].

In this contribution, we report a new strategy for the simple and high output synthesis of high-quality GQDs by solvothermal treatment of layered Ti3C2Tx MXene in dimethylformamide (DMF) in 2 h' time, which is much shorter than other reported reaction duration (Fig. 1 ) [16–18]. MXene (e.g., Ti3C2Tx) is a typical kind of two-dimensional material and is produced by the extraction of the A-element from the corresponding MAX phase (e.g., Ti3AlC2) [19–22]. Note that MXene surface is terminated by O, OH, and/or F groups after etching process in HF solution, so herein the “T” in Ti3C2Tx represents terminal groups (O, OH, and/or F) and x is the number of terminal groups [20,21]. Compared with the previously reported methods, our strategy possesses several obvious merits: 1) safety and convenience by elimination of strong acid treatment and post-modification steps, 2) high product yield (32.6%) using rapid reaction process and 3) high QY value (12.4%) for the resulted GQDs with amphiphilicity. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on synthesis of GQDs from MXene.

Ti3AlC2 (98%) was purchased from Forsman Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). HF solution (48 wt%), acrylamide (AAm), methylene-bis-acrylamide (MBA), tetramethylenediamine (TEMED), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 3500), quinine sulfate, and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received. All other chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Mill-Q water (18.2 MΩ) was used throughout the experiments, which was purified with a Millipore system.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100 F high-resolution TEM at 200 kV. Samples were prepared by placing a drop of dilute aqueous dispersion on the surface of a copper grid. Atom force microscope (AFM) images were collected by a NT MDT (Ntegra Prima) SPM, operating at tapping mode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using JSM-6380 LV microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a FTIR 2500 spectrometer (KBr disk). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were determined on a Holland PANalytical X-Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Kratos AXIS UltraDLD (Kratos Analytical Ltd) with mono Al Kα radiation (hν = 1487.71 eV) at a power of 75 W. Raman spectra were collected on a LabRam-1B Raman spectroscope equipped with a 632.8 nm laser source. Emission spectra were collected using a Varian Cary 100 spectrometer. Absorption spectra were measured on a UV-3600 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Photoluminescence quantum yields were determined using a Hamamatsu absolute photoluminescence quantum yield spectrometer C11347 Quantaurus-QY. Confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were recorded on a Zeiss LSM 510 (Jena, Germany) CLSM.

The multilayer Ti3C2Tx MXene was prepared according to the literature with minor revision [20]. Briefly, 5.0 g of Ti3AlC2 powder was immersed in 80 mL of HF solution (48 wt%) at room temperature for 5 h. The resulting suspension was then washed several times using water and centrifuged to separate the black powder (multilayer Ti3C2Tx MXene).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from Ti3C2Tx MXene
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 100 mg of multilayer Ti3C2Tx MXene was added into 100 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and the mixture was treated with ultrasonication bath (700 W) for 3 h. After removal of large particles by centrifugation, the resulting colloidal suspension of delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 2 h. The resulted brownish yellow dispersion was centrifuged at 3500 rpm/min for 5 min to remove the deposit. Subsequently, the supernatant was dialysed (molecular weight cut off, 500 Da) against water for 24 h. Finally, the resulted GQDs were dispersed in water as stock solution for further characterization and application. For calculation of product yield, stoichiometric GQDs aqueous solution was dried and weighed. Elemental analysis result of the GQDs: C 64.02 wt%, H 6.51 wt%, N 7.73 wt% and O 21.74 wt% (calculated).

The product yield value was calculated according to the following equation:
(1) product yield = (mass of C in obtained GQD)/(mass of C in delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene)

For fluorescent GQDs ink, 6 mL aqueous dispersion of GQDs (2.32 mg/mL) was blended with glycol (5 g) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) (0.5 g) to form a homogeneous mixture, which was encapsulated into the ink cartridge of HP DeskJet1111 inkjet printer for printing at room temperature. For pen-on-paper process, the mixture was loaded into an empty pen tube, with which we can write fluorescent words on a paper.

For the synthesis of fluorescent PAAm-GQDs hydrogel, two solutions were prepared. Solution A contains GQDs, AAm, MBA, and TEMED. The concentration of GQDs, AAm, MBA and TEMED were maintained in 0.4 mg/mL, 3 M, 40 mM, and 45 mM, respectively. Solution B contains K2S2O8 in the concentration of 65 mM. Then 9 mL of solution A and 1 mL of solution B were mixed and the mixture was quickly cast to a glass plate. After keeping the system at room temperature for 8 h, fluorescent PAAm-GQDs hydrogel was obtained.

In a typical procedure, MCF-7 breast cancer cells were cultured in a chamber at 37 °C. After 80% confluence, the medium was removed and the adherent cells were washed thrice with 1 × PBS buffer. The GQDs suspension (20 μg/mL, 0.5 mL) was then added to the chamber. After incubation for 2 h, cells were washed three times with 1 × PBS buffer and then fixed by 75% ethanol for 20 min, which was further washed twice with 1 × PBS buffer and imaged by CLSM with imaging software.

Human embryo kidney cells 293T and MCF-7 cancer cells were chosen to evaluate the cytotoxicity of GQDs based on methyl-thiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium (MTT) viability assay [23,24]. The 293T and MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 4 × 104 cells/mL, respectively. After 24 h incubation, the medium was replaced by GQDs with concentrations of 400, 200, 100, 50, and 0 mg/mL and further incubated for 24 h. After the designated time intervals, the wells were washed thrice with PBS buffer, and 100 μL of freshly prepared MTT (0.5 mg/mL) solution in culture medium was added to each well. The MTT medium solution was carefully removed after 3 h incubation. Dimethyl sulphoxide (100 μL) was then added into each well, and the plate was gently shaken for 20 min. The absorbance of MTT at 570 nm was recorded by the microplate reader. Cell viability was expressed by the ratio of absorbance of the cells incubated with GQDs to that of the cells incubated with culture medium only.

Before solvothermal reaction, the multilayered Ti3C2Tx MXene (Fig. 2 a) was exfoliated by simple sonication treatment in DMF. The delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene shows excellent dispersibility in DMF (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atom force microscope (AFM) images demonstrated that single- or few-layers Ti3C2Tx flakes with lateral sizes ranging from hundreds of nanometers to several micrometers (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1) were formed. The successful delamination of Ti3C2Tx MXene was also confirmed by the XRD characterization according to the literature (Fig. S1) [20]. Owing to the formation of GQDs, the black DMF dispersion of Ti3C2Tx MXene after solvothermal treatment became brownish yellow and transparent in daylight but emit intense blue photoluminescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1b and c). Notably, the product yield of GQDs in this work can reach as high as 32.6%, which compares favorably to the previous reports [16–18]. It should be pointed that the direct solvothermal treatment of non-delaminated Ti3C2Tx can also lead to formation of GQDs but with low product yield (11.4%). The influence of reaction time on the fluorescence property of GQDs was investigated and found that the reaction was completed within about 2 h (Fig. S2). Such high reaction rate is possibly attributed to the presence of weak CTi bonds, which are more prone to break, instead of strong CC bonds in Ti3C2Tx MXene [20]. It should be pointed that the direct solvothermal treatment of DMF at 200 °C can not afford any fluorescent products (Fig. S2).

TEM images (Fig. 2c and Fig. S3) indicate that the sizes of as-made GQDs are in the range of 4–10 nm. From the corresponding high-resolution TEM images, most GQDs are observed to be crystalline with a lattice parameter of 0.215 nm, which agrees with (100) lattice fringes of graphene [25]; and rare particles are amorphous carbon nanoparticles without any lattices (Fig. 2c and Fig. S3). This result indicates that the carbon atoms in MXene could assemble into larger graphitic structures during the solvothermal reaction process. The AFM images and corresponding height analysis reveal a topographic height distribution between 0.68 and 2.74 nm with maximum population at 1.36 nm (Fig. 2d and Fig. S3), suggesting that most of GQDs consist of two graphene layers.

The Raman spectra of the delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene and GQDs are shown in Fig. 3 a. After solvothermal reaction, the peaks corresponding to the Ti component disappear and two characteristic D and G bands of graphitic structure at 1365 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, which are ascribed respectively to disordered carbon and ordered sp2 carbon, can be clearly observed. The relative intensity of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) is 0.94, which is supportive of the partial amorphous nature of the GQDs and consistent with the TEM results. Compared with delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene, two strong bands at 2852 and 2926 cm−1 corresponding to CH stretching, and four obvious bands at 1722, 1638, 1467 and 1086 cm−1 respectively associated with CO, CC, CN and CO groups appeared in the FTIR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 3b). Simultaneous presence of polar and nonpolar functional groups can endow the GQDs with excellent solubility both in hydrophilic solvents (e.g., DMF, water, ethanol, and tetrahydrofuran (THF)) and in hydrophobic solvents (e.g., chloroform and toluene) even without further chemical modification.

Compared with the delaminated Ti3C2Tx MXene, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of GQDs only shows a dominant graphitic C 1s peak at ca. 284.6 eV, N 1s peak at ca. 399.5 eV, and O 1s peak at ca. 531.4 eV (Fig. 3c). The elemental analysis result indicated that the composition of GQDs was C 64.02 wt%, H 6.51 wt%, N 7.73 wt% and O 21.74 wt% (calculated). The disappearance of Ti and F peaks is caused by the etching of Ti layer from the Ti3C2Tx MXene (Fig. 3d). In order to probe the whereabouts of Ti component after solvothermal reaction, the deposit sediment at the bottom of Teflon-lined autoclave was analyzed. The results indicate that antase TiO2 nanoparticles with irregular shape and size in the range of 30–90 nm were formed (Fig. S4). Consistent with the FT-IR result, the corresponding high-resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3e reveals that GQDs contain CO, CO and CN bonds. The presence of N 1s peak is introduced by the nitrogen-doping process, since N element does not exist in the untreated Ti3C2Tx MXene. It is reported that DMF could be decomposed to dimethylamine and carbon monoxide at temperature higher than its boiling point (Fig. S5a) [26]. We deduce that the dimethylamine are formed during the solvothermal process and it can react with the carboxylic groups of GQDs (Fig. S5b). Moreover, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum of GQDs reveals the existence of both CN (399.8 eV) and C(O)N (401.7 eV) bonds (Fig. 3f), further confirming the presence of nitrogen-doping process [26]. On the other hand, the strong fluorescence of GQDs is attributed to the nitrogen-doping process since the nitrogen-doping can significantly enhance the fluorescent performance of carbon nanomaterials [27,28]. As control experiments, other solvents including water, ethanol, chloroform and THF were also employed as reaction media for the hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment of Ti3C2Tx MXene, whereas no obvious fluorescent products were obtained (Fig. S6). XPS result indicates that the products prepared in other solvents did not contain N element (Fig. S6c). Accordingly, DMF in this case can simultaneously work as reaction media and nitrogen precursor for achieving nitrogen-doped GQDs. Although the accurate mechanism for the formation of highly fluorescent GQDs from Ti3C2Tx MXene in DMF is still unclear at present, we could deduce the possible reaction mechanism based on the characterization results and previous reports. The solvothermal treatment can break the CTi bonds of Ti3C2Tx MXene and the resulted carbon atoms may self-assemble into GQDs with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups like other reported GQDs [18]. Subsequently, the dimethylamine derived from DMF can react with the carboxylic groups of GQDs to form highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped GQDs [26].

Benefiting from their amphiphilic characteristic, the GQDs can emit strong fluorescence both in hydrophilic solvents (e.g., DMF, water, ethanol and THF) and in hydrophobic solvents (e.g., chloroform and toluene) (Fig. 4 a). The optical properties of the GQDs in water were investigated in detail. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the GQDs show an obvious absorption peak at 235 nm and a shoulder peak at 322 nm. The peak at 235 nm is ascribed to the π−π* transition of CC bonds, whereas the peak at 322 nm is possibly caused by the n−π* transition of the CO bonds on the surface of GQDs [16,28]. Similar to other reported GQDs, the as-prepared GQDs also exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviors (Fig. 4c) [29–33]. When the GQDs are excited from 300 to 440 nm, the corresponding emission peaks shift from 390 nm to 490 nm. The strongest emission peak appears at 405 nm and the absolute QY value of GQDs can reach 12.4%. To probe the origin of fluorescence, the fluorescence lifetime (τ) of GQDs was evaluated (Fig. 4d). The fluorescence decay trace can be fitted by a triple-exponential functions Y(t) according to the following equation:
(2)
Y
( t )
=
A 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
+
A 3
exp
( − t /
τ 3
)
where A1, A2, and A3 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1, τ2, and τ3, respectively. The average lifetime (τav) of the GQDs was calculated to be 5.98 ns (see Supplementary Material), which is comparable to the reported values [34]. Moreover, the GQDs are very stable in aqueous media, and no precipitate is observed even after being stored at room temperature for three months. Meanwhile, the GQDs show stable aqueous photoluminescence behaviors against ionic strength variation and pH change (Fig. 4e and f), indicating their suitability to be used in complex environments.

On the basis of their excellent dispersibility and strong photoluminescence in various solvents, we have conveniently demonstrated the utilization of the GQDs to fabricate fluorescent GQDs ink and light-emitting composites. First, the GQDs were blended with glycol and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP). The mixture with low content of GQDs (0.15 wt %) is colorless under daylight but show bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5 a). It can be directly used as ink to write fluorescent words (Fig. 5b). For printing application, the mixture was injected into a vacant cartridge from a commercial inkjet printer and a highly fluorescent “Monkey” image can be easily printed (Fig. 5c).

In addition, the GQDs can also be utilized as fluorescent nanofillers for fabricating light-emitting composites. Through simple blending of the GQDs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in water and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) in chloroform and followed by evaporation of solvents, PVA-GQDs and PMMA-GQDs composite films were obtained. The obtained films with high transparency exhibit strong fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6 a and b), suggesting that the optical properties of the GQDs are well retained during the blending and drying process. In contrast, the optical properties of many other fluorescent agents (e.g., semiconductor quantum dots) are susceptible to external environment and may completely lose their fluorescence during post-process or chemical reaction. To our delight, our GQDs retained their fluorescent properties even after employing for synthesizing fluorescent polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogel (Fig. 6c, d and Fig. S7). Moreover, the as-prepared films and hydrogels showed high photostability with no obvious decrease of photoluminescence even under the UV light (365 nm) for 24 h or under daylight over three months and transparency can be maintained completely.

Considering their high fluorescent QY and excellent water dispersibility, the GQDs were also demonstrated as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging application (Fig. 7 a–c). Breast cancer cells MCF-7 were incubated with aqueous dispersion of GQDs (20 μg/mL) for 2 h and then images were taken under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As the GQDs could show excitation-dependent fluorescence, strong green and blue fluorescence were observed for MCF-7 cells by laser excitation at 488 and 364 nm, respectively, suggesting that the GQDs were efficiently internalized by MCF-7 cells (Fig. 7a–c). A z-axis imaging of the stained MCF-7 cells (Fig. S8) further confirmed the internalization of GQDs into the cell cytoplasm rather than bounded or adsorbed on the cell surface. In addition, no fluorescence was observed for the cells without incubation with GQDs (Fig. S9). The photostability of GQDs was evaluated by continuous CLSM laser illumination for 10 min. For comparison, commercially available fluorescent agents Alexa fluo 488 and fluorescein were also studied under the same condition. As presented in Fig. 7d, no obvious fluorescence decline (<5%) was observed for GQDs, whereas the fluorescent intensities decreased by 84% and 24% for fluorescein and Alexa fluo 488, respectively.

As low toxicity is essential for bioapplications, the cytotoxicity of GQDs was examined in human embryo kidney cells 293T and MCF-7 cancer cells using methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) viability assay. The cell viabilities of both 293T and MCF-7 cells were close to 100% at 50 and 100 μg/mL and remain close to 90% at 400 μg/mL within the tested period (Fig. 8 ), indicating the low cytotoxicity of GQDs. It is noteworthy that the concentration of GQDs used for cellular imaging (20 μg/mL) is much lower than that of MTT assay. Accordingly, the GQDs hold great promise as a fluorescent probe for bioimaging due to their low toxicity, strong and stable fluorescence.

In summary, we have developed a solvothermal method to prepare high-quality carbide-derived GQDs using Ti3C2Tx MXene as a novel and effective precursor. It was found that the solvent media DMF could act simultaneously as nitrogen-doping agent resulting in highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped GQDs. The as-synthesized GQDs are rich in surface functional groups and amphiphilic, rendering them with excellent solubility in water and most organic solvents. Moreover, the GQDs have stable optical properties irrespective to ionic strength or pH change and much higher photostability as compared to Alexa fluo 488 and fluorescein. Together with their low cytotoxicity, the GQDs hold great promise for various optical applications including fluorescent ink, light-emitting composites and bioimaging. The novel strategy of cutting C-metal bonds to afford GQDs or fluorescent carbide-derived carbons could be applied to other layered MXene materials, thus this work opens a new and exciting avenue in the development of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21364003 and No. 51663007), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (Grant No. 2014GXNSFCA118004), and project of outstanding young teachers' training in higher education institutions of Guangxi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.03.023.
